content.full_text:
+1 5

RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

June 17, 1996

Alice Calvert
City Clerk
City of San Leandro
835 E. 14th Street
San Leandro, CA 94577

Re: Your Request for Assistance
Our File No. I-96-132

Dear Ms. Calvert:

This is in response to your request for assistance under the
campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act.1/ Since you are
merely seeking guidance concerning the application of the act's
provisions to an organization within your community, we are
treating your letter as a request for informal assistance.2/

This letter will also confirm our telephone conversation of
Monday, May 20, 1996.

QUESTION

Is the group "San Leandrans for Responsible Government" a
committee subject to the campaign disclosure provisions of the
Political Reform Act?

_________________________

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2/ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs. Section
18329(c)(3).)

428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660

Page 2
File No. I-96-132


CONCLUSION

No. San Leandrans for Responsible Government is not an
organization formed or existing primarily for political purposes.
It has not received campaign contributions totaling $1,000 or more
or made independent expenditures. Thus, it is not subject to the
campaign reporting provisions of the Act.

FACTS

San Leandrans for Responsible Government ("SLRG") describes
itself as an alliance of home owners and business owners formed to
educate citizens, businesses, and industry leaders on city and
county government issues which may affect them. Just prior to the
March 1996 primary election, SLRG mailed a newsletter and
published advertisements in the San Leandro Times to promote its
cause. To fund their activities, SLRG received approximately
$5,400 from citizens and businesses.

You provided me with copies of the advertisements published,
as well as a newsletter sent to the citizens of San Leandro.

The newsletter addressed to "All San Leandrans" describes the
formation of SLRG and describes the mission of the organization.
It states as some of its purposes that its goals are:

1) To unite home owners, commerce, and industry into a
common voice for the good of the alliance and all the people that
live and work in the city.

2) To produce and provide timely informative bulletins,
letters, and news releases to all residents, workers, and
businesses on the pros and cons of pending city and county
expenditures, developments, legislation, and plans that impact
taxes and fees, the short and long term quality of life and the
costs of living and working in San Leandro.

3) To provide an open and accessible forum for even the
smallest business, individual home owner or renter, and any member
of the alliance.

4) To retain and support city controlled qualified police
and fire services - free of politics.

5) To solicit and raise funds from businesses, property
owners, and residents for the above purposes.

In addition to the newsletter, SLRG published several paid
advertisements in the San Leandro Times. A February 8, 1996,
advertisement appears to be a solicitation for financial support.
This advertisement states the philosophies and goals of SLRG,
lists its officers and their telephone numbers, encourages
communication between elected officials and their electorate, and
suggests solutions to some of the problems the city is facing.

Page 3
File No. I-96-132


The February 29, 1996, advertisement is a retraction of a
statement SLRG made in a previous advertisement relating to pay
increases to all city employees.  The advertisement also provides
the names and telephone numbers of all city council candidates and
encourages readers to call their candidates to ask their opinion
on specific issues.

The March 7, 1996, advertisement discusses an issue regarding
the costs of publishing the city's "San Leandro Quarterly" paper,
lists a new telephone number for a city council candidate, and
contains a reprint of letters from two citizens supporting the
efforts of SLRG.

The March 14, 1996, advertisement lists the names of the
candidates and provides a "brief observation" of the
qualifications or experience of each candidate.  None of the
observations encourage citizens to vote for, or against, any named
candidate.  The statements are neutral and provide both positive,
as well as negative comments for each candidate.  For instance,
for candidate Joanne Lothrop, the observation reads: "Limited
civic experience, unaware of some issues.  Supports some changes
but also supports present City administration."

The March 21, 1996, advertisement contains messages prepared
by the candidates which SLRG paid to have published in the San
Leandro Times.  Nine candidates were invited to submit a 60- to
70-word statement.  Seven of the candidates accepted SLRG's
invitation.  Since SLRG, not the candidates, paid for the
advertisement, SLRG did provide a benefit to each candidate
listed.  A.J. Rosenga, Jr., the treasurer for SLRG, informed me by
phone that the total cost of this advertisement was $300.

SLRG, believing itself to be a committee subject to the
disclosure provisions of the Act, filed a statement of
organization on March 26, 1996, and filed a campaign disclosure
statement designed for use by a ballot measure committee.

ANALYSIS

The Political Reform Act requires the disclosure of
contributions received and expenditures made by campaign
committees.  The purpose of such disclosure is to fully and
truthfully inform the voters of sources of campaign contributions
during elections.

A "committee" means any person or combination of persons who
directly or indirectly does any of the following:

(a)  Receives contributions totaling one thousand
dollars ($1,000) or more in a calendar year.

(b)  Makes independent expenditures totaling one
thousand dollars ($1,000) or more in a calendar year; or

Page 4
File No. I-96-132


(c) Makes contributions totaling ten thousand
dollars ($10,000) or more in a calendar year to or at
the behest of candidates or committees.

Section 82013.

The term "contribution" as used above, means "a payment, a
forgiveness of a loan, a payment of a loan by a third party, or an
enforceable promise to make a payment...unless it is clear from
the surrounding circumstances that it is not made for political
purposes..." (Section 82015.)

A payment is made for political purposes if it is:

(1) For the purpose of influencing or attempting
to influence the action of the voters for or against the
nomination or election of a candidate or candidates, or
the qualification or passage of any measure; or

(2) Received by or made at the behest of the
following or any agent thereof:

(A) A candidate;

* * *

(D) An organization formed or existing
primarily for political purposes, including, but not
limited to, a political action committee established by
any membership organization, labor union or corporation.

Regulation 18215(a).

An "independent expenditure" as used in Section 82013, means
an expenditure made by any person in connection with a
communication which expressly advocates the election or defeat of
a clearly identified candidate or the qualification, passage or
defeat of a clearly identified measure, or taken as a whole and in
context, unambiguously urges a particular result in an election
but which is not made to or at the behest of the affected
candidate or committee.

Section 82031.

The activities of SLRG could be construed by some as
primarily for political purposes. Granted SLRG was actively
soliciting money from businesses and citizens to be used to
encourage voters to get involved in an upcoming election, but
virtually all of the advertisements requested citizens to take
some independent action to contact candidates directly, to voice
their opinions on various issues before the city council or to
educate themselves on how their representatives voted on issues of

Page 5
File No. I-96-132


importance to them. None of the advertisements attempted to
influence voters for, or against, the election of a clearly
identified candidate or measure.

     The March 14, 1996, advertisement which summarized SLRG's
observations of every candidate was fairly neutral. The
observations summarized the qualifications, experience, positive
and negative attributes of every candidate and did not urge voters
to vote for, or against, any candidate. The language in the March
14, 1996, advertisement did not qualify as an independent
expenditure under Section 82031.

     The March 21, 1996, advertisement, which contained the 60-
word statements prepared by seven city council candidates, is the
only activity which could qualify SLRG as a committee. SLRG paid
for the advertisement on behalf of each candidate. Each candidate
did receive a benefit of the space in the advertisement which
contained his or her respective statement. The advertisement was
a full-page ad which dedicated only the top half to the
candidates' summaries. The bottom half of the advertisement
addresses various city government issues.

     Mr. Rosenga advised that the cost of the entire advertisement
was $300. Only that portion of the advertisement which provided a
benefit to each candidate would be deemed a contribution. Some
method of reasonable valuation is necessary to determine the value
of the benefit to each candidate. For instance, since only one-
half of the advertisement was used for the candidates' statements,
then only one-half of the total cost would be attributed as an
expenditure for political purposes. That one-half value of the
advertisement ($150) could be divided by the seven candidates who
received a benefit. Using this method, the estimated value of the
contribution to each candidate is $21. While this amount does
represent a contribution to each candidate, it does not represent
a contribution which must be itemized on the candidates' campaign
statements.3/

     Accordingly, SLRG did not qualify as a committee as defined
under Section 82013. The March 21 advertisement did convey a
contribution to each candidate listed, but is well below the
$1,000 threshold to qualify SLRG as a committee at this time.

     Mr. Rosenga has been contacted and advised that the
activities of SLRG did not qualify it as a committee under the
provisions of the Act. He was further advised to file a statement
of termination, along with a letter summarizing our telephone

3/
     The Act requires candidates and committees to itemize each
contribution of $100 or more, disclosing the name and address of
the source, employer or occupation information, or if self-
employed, the name of the business, be disclosed, as well as the
cumulative amount of contributions received from that source.
(Section 84211.)

Page 6
File No. I-96-132


conversation.  You should be receiving a copy of the
organization's statement of termination from Mr. Rosenga shortly.

     If I can assist you further, or if you have any questions,
please feel free to call me at (916) 322-5660.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Steven G. Churchwell
                                        General Counsel

                                        [signature]
                                        By:  Jeanette E. Turvill
                                             Political Reform Consultant
                                             Technical Assistance Division

SGC/JET/jt

cc.  A.J. Rosenga, Jr.

No.
San Leandrans for Responsible Government is not an
organization formed or existing primarily for political purposes.
It has not received campaign contributions totaling $1,000 or more
or made independent expenditures.
Thus, it is not subject to the
campaign reporting provisions of the Act.
